result:
A shoreline is a place where maps apologize. The ink comes right up to the surf and then lifts its hands. Everything steady becomes almost, the way a chair is when someone just stood from it, the ghost of a sitting still warm in the wood. I grew up inland, with lake horizons that faked ocean, and so the first time I saw the tide pull a line of wrack backwards I believed, briefly, that the world had learned to breathe.

There’s a lighthouse keeper in my head who trims wicks that don’t exist. He moves in circles, polishing lenses, setting the pendulum of a clock that always loses a few honest seconds to the wind. He knows that light is only useful if the weather decides to allow it. He carries a thermos of coffee that tastes like burnt hope and keeps a notebook of ships that never asked to be saved.

In the city, morning collects in low places. The gutter holds a river of leaf-brown tea. A delivery truck makes a sound like backing into a mistake. Someone slips a croissant into a paper bag and the paper breathes. Cyclists arc around parked cars as if they’ve been told a secret about angles and mercy. A small dog wears a sweater knitted by somebody’s grandmother’s hands, those hands that now probably smell like orange peels, lotion, and coin.

My mother could whistle into a bottle in a way that made the kitchen sound haunted. She called it borrowing the room from the room. Wind in a glass, breath in a mouth, notes that do not know where they’re going but go anyway. When she was tired she would hum the same three notes over and over, the simplest lighthouse. I think that’s what love looks like from far away: a repeated pattern you’re grateful for even when you’ve stopped naming it.

If memory has a unit, it’s neither minute nor photograph. It’s the small hitch you hear when a friend says it’s fine and you know it isn’t. It’s the way your fingers remember the soft divot on the seventh stair where everyone in your family put their weight. It’s the light ring left by a glass on a table that woodenly forgives you. Memory is what you can recite with your eyes closed, that prayer you learned when you needed to pretend to be holy.

Another unit of measurement: the world by its quiets. The quiet between two people at a bus stop who both know they will never see each other again. The quiet inside a museum when the security guard coughs in the wing of ancient pottery. The quiet where a spider remakes what somebody else walked through. The quiet in an elevator where a child reads each button like a constellation.

I keep wanting to write an instruction manual for being lost. Step one: notice what still knows where it is. The coffee shop that smells exactly like a Tuesday in late March. The plant that leans toward any light without apology. The old man reading the newspaper who sighs like a kettle on low. Step two: carry a safety pin, a bandage, and one sentence you believe enough to set down under your foot. Step three: when you ask for directions, admit that you are not just looking for a street.

In the library, I once found a note in a book that said: This page always makes me feel less alone. The page was about a woman learning to peel an orange without breaking the peel. I didn’t cry, but something inside me arranged itself as if it had been crying and now it could stop. There should be a word for that. There should be a word for opening a drawer and finding a smell of winter.

If you could only keep five smells: rain on hot asphalt, cut grass on your calves, bread cracking open, the sweat of a person you love after they have run to meet you, and your own shoulder in a sweater after you have been somewhere worth being. I suppose pine, tomatoes on the vine, the salt-metal of the sea might argue their case. I suppose the ghosts of all unlucky onions would say, Hey now.

I write letters I do not send. Dear Autumn, you are very good at orange and I admire that about you, but sometimes you mistake my face for a field. Dear First Mosquito of Summer, I know it isn’t personal, but my blood is so very narrow in my veins; be kind. Dear Person Three Seats Up on the Bus Who Dropped a Photograph, you should know the boy in the picture has a smile that looks like a kite finally getting air. I tucked it into the space between the seats so it could wait for you like a faithful dog.

Sometimes I want to be a bench. Just a place that holds what it is asked to hold until the question changes. A bench doesn’t mind the rain. It knows it cannot go inside. It knows that eventually the sun will warm the slats again and there will be gum under it and initials in it and a light dust of pollen making everything yellow and holy.

At the museum near closing, the paintings yawn. You can’t hear it unless you let your eyes unfocus slightly and admit how good they are at pretending to be windows. The guards exchange gossip in hand signals. The marble thinks about glaciers. Somewhere, a child touches a velvet rope and feels like a heist.

I have pressed my ear to a seashell and heard not the ocean but my own waiting. I have put a glass over a wasp, slid a postcard underneath, and carried the furious, beautiful thing outside, where it took off as if it had never been inside anything. I have pretended to know the names of stars and then been forgiven by the night for lying.

There are days when my mind is a kitchen after a party. There are days when it is a meadow / a guitar case / a sink full of sky. On the good ones, I make a soup called Staying. Ingredients: whatever you have, cut into bite-sized pieces. Salt enough to taste like what you’ve been through. Heat until everything is softer than it was. Share without apologizing for the recipe.

What do we do with the spare minutes? The ones you get when the train is four minutes late, when the kettle takes a breath before it boils, when the meeting could have been an email and wasn’t. You could answer a text with a real question. You could look at your hands and say, Thank you, you odd soft tools. You could learn the name of a bird not because names matter to the bird but because saying them makes your mouth a better room to live in.

A friend told me the color of almost is that moment right before the streetlights turn on in summer. You can see everything and nothing is illuminated. I think almost is a virtue. I think we treat arriving like a trophy when all the trophies I know gather dust and all the almosts I know teach me to adjust my eyes.

A shoreline is a place where we practice not owning anything. The water keeps what it wants. The sand keeps what can’t escape it. We keep stepping forward and the earth steps back. Somewhere a lighthouse keeper is winding a clock that will betray him in the smallest, truest way. Somewhere, a map is being drawn in pencil so that it can be corrected when the tide proves the cartographer human.

Tonight I will go to sleep listening for the soft click of the world correcting itself. If I cannot hear it, I will pretend a radio is scanning the sky, finding the music of distant storms, the static of half-heard advice, the heartbeat someone left on a voicemail by accident. I will think of a bench holding a coat and the shape of someone inside it. I will think of an orange peel coming off in one long animal skin. I will think of a word that means the relief after not crying, and I will not know it, and I will be fine.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
